critic_raw:
GAN2Shape rests on the assumption that varying the latent vector w in StyleGAN2 produces geometrically valid viewpoint and lighting changes. StyleGAN2 was trained on 2D image collections with no 3D supervision or multi-view consistency objective. Its latent traversals encode learned correlations between texture, color, and appearance — not verified 3D geometric transformations. A latent interpolation that looks like a viewpoint change may be a learned texture hallucination with no geometric grounding. The method does not verify that the pseudo-views generated by latent manipulation satisfy any geometric consistency constraint before using them as pseudo-supervision.

The evaluation scope covers only four object categories — human faces, cats, cars, and buildings — all of which are heavily represented in StyleGAN2's training data. The method relies directly on the learned priors of StyleGAN2 for these specific domains. Any claim about general 3D extraction capability is unsupported by this experimental scope; the results may reflect category-specific priors rather than a general geometric principle.

Shape quality is assessed through downstream rendered image quality (re-lighting, rotation) rather than quantitative comparison against ground-truth 3D measurements. A visually plausible-looking result under a flexible learned lighting model does not confirm veridical geometry. In particular, near-flat depth maps with a learnable albedo and flexible lighting could produce convincing re-lit images for frontal objects, especially faces, without recovering meaningful 3D structure.
all_issues_raised: StyleGAN2 latent traversals encode 2D appearance correlations without 3D supervision; the assumption that w-space manipulations correspond to geometrically consistent viewpoint changes is unverified.
Evaluation restricted to four categories heavily represented in StyleGAN2 training data; general 3D extraction claims are unsupported by the experimental scope.
Shape evaluation uses rendered image quality as a proxy rather than ground-truth 3D metrics, leaving flat depth-map artifacts undetectable.

round_transcripts.critic:
GAN2Shape rests on the assumption that varying the latent vector w in StyleGAN2 produces geometrically valid viewpoint and lighting changes. StyleGAN2 was trained on 2D image collections with no 3D supervision or multi-view consistency objective. Its latent traversals encode learned correlations between texture, color, and appearance — not verified 3D geometric transformations. A latent interpolation that looks like a viewpoint change may be a learned texture hallucination with no geometric grounding. The method does not verify that the pseudo-views generated by latent manipulation satisfy any geometric consistency constraint before using them as pseudo-supervision.

The evaluation scope covers only four object categories — human faces, cats, cars, and buildings — all of which are heavily represented in StyleGAN2's training data. The method relies directly on the learned priors of StyleGAN2 for these specific domains. Any claim about general 3D extraction capability is unsupported by this experimental scope; the results may reflect category-specific priors rather than a general geometric principle.

Shape quality is assessed through downstream rendered image quality (re-lighting, rotation) rather than quantitative comparison against ground-truth 3D measurements. A visually plausible-looking result under a flexible learned lighting model does not confirm veridical geometry. In particular, near-flat depth maps with a learnable albedo and flexible lighting could produce convincing re-lit images for frontal objects, especially faces, without recovering meaningful 3D structure.
round_transcripts.defender:
The latent-space critique partially mischaracterizes the method. GAN2Shape does not claim that w-space traversals are geometrically exact 3D rotations. Instead, it exploits the observation that StyleGAN2 generates perceptually plausible multi-view and multi-lighting renderings of objects when the latent code is varied — these are used as pseudo-training signal for a separate shape network and differentiable renderer. The 3D geometry is inferred by the shape network to explain all pseudo-views simultaneously under a physically grounded rendering equation. The quality of geometry recovery thus depends on the self-consistency of the pseudo-views across the full set of latent manipulations, not on any single latent traversal being geometrically exact.

The domain scope is a legitimate experimental design choice, not a hidden generalization claim. The paper explicitly requires a pre-trained StyleGAN2 model and is evaluated on available pre-trained domains. The claim is that the method extracts 3D shape from 2D GANs — demonstrated on four well-defined categories — not that it works for arbitrary categories without a suitable GAN.

On evaluation: for fully unsupervised methods applied to in-the-wild images, 3D ground truth is unavailable. Re-lighting and rotation quality serve as proxy evaluations that are standard for this class of unsupervised methods. If the recovered depth map were flat, off-axis lighting would produce physically incorrect shadow gradients and the rotation would create implausible texture distortions — the proxy is imperfect but functional for non-trivially non-planar objects.

round_transcripts.critic:
The defender's correction that 3D geometry is inferred by a separate shape network rather than read from latent space directly is a meaningful clarification. However, the shape network's supervision signal is the set of pseudo-views from the GAN, and if those pseudo-views contain inconsistent geometric information — e.g., the GAN produces texture blends instead of true viewpoint rotations — the shape network optimizes to a depth map that reconciles geometrically inconsistent pseudo-labels. Self-consistency among pseudo-labels does not imply geometric correctness; it implies the shape network has found an equilibrium that explains the GAN's outputs, which may themselves be geometrically incorrect.

The proxy evaluation argument fails for near-planar categories. Human faces have limited depth variation relative to texture variation. A learned albedo with a flexible per-pixel lighting model can explain most of the appearance variation in face pseudo-views without committing to non-trivial depth structure. The re-lighting test is only geometrically constraining if the illumination direction produces shadow gradients that require non-trivial geometry to explain — this is less true for diffuse, low-relief surfaces like faces.
round_transcripts.defender:
The geometric inconsistency concern in pseudo-view supervision is real for categories where StyleGAN2's view synthesis is less accurate. The iterative training loop provides partial mitigation: the shape network and the view synthesis are jointly optimized to be mutually consistent, which filters out pseudo-views that are not reconcilable with any plausible 3D shape. However, this self-consistency constraint does not rule out systematic biases — if the GAN consistently produces shallower depth variation than the true geometry, the shape network will systematically underestimate depth. This is a genuine limitation.

The near-planar face argument is the strongest residual critique. It is accepted that the re-lighting proxy is less constraining for low-relief surfaces. For cars, cats, and buildings — which have more pronounced 3D structure — the proxy provides stronger geometric grounding. The paper's evaluation does not separately analyze recovery quality by object depth complexity, which would be informative.

all_issues_adjudicated: Pseudo-view geometric inconsistency: GAN latent traversals may produce systematically biased depth estimates; self-consistency constraint does not guarantee geometric accuracy.
Near-planar evaluation failure: re-lighting proxy does not provide meaningful 3D constraints for faces; flat depth maps cannot be ruled out from reported metrics.
